question_1:
Immediate settlement or Elastic settlement (Se)

Compression of dry soil, moist and saturated soil without change in w
Happens immediately after load is applied

This volume change is completely reversible

Elastic settlement calculation is based on theory of elasticity

This is not a significant settlement
question_2:
Primary consolidation settlement (Sp)

Volume change/ settlement/ compression in a saturated soil due to the expulsion of water from the voids
This volume change is irreversible and happens due to plastic re-arrangement

This settlement depends on the permeability of soil and the drainage condition existing in the field
Therefore, this settlement is time dependent

This settlement can be significant depending on soil and thereby resulting in the failure of structures
question_3:
Secondary consolidation settlement (Ss)

Volume change or settlement in saturated clays after primary consolidation settlement is completed

The volume change is irreversible and happens under constant effective stress (creep behaviour)

Not as significant as Sp
Difficult to separate the end of Sp and beginning of Ss (both overlap)

Occurs mostly in highly compressive or highly organic clays
Total soil settlement

Total settlement St = Se + Sp + Ss

For sandy soils, elastic settlement is more because of better drainage (happens instantly)
For clayey soils, consolidation settlement will be significant

It is difficult to assess Sp and Ss at the time of construction

The knowledge of consolidation behavior of soil is needed to determine Sp and Ss

Consolidation behavior is determined for undisturbed soil subjected to load

Elastic or immediate settlement (Se)

Consolidation
The decrease in volume by expelling excess pore water pressure induced by an imposed total stress

There are two aspects of consolidation behavior
a) Compressibility of the soil (Total primary consolidation the soil undergoes)
b) Time dependent consolidation (in what time the primary consolidation occur (rate of consolidation))

Time

Major volume change is due to Sp Elastic compression

Primary consolidation

Settlement

Secondary compression
Fundamentals of consolidation

When a saturated fine grained soil is acted upon by external pressure, the pore water pressure increase
instantly

The increase in u due to external load is excess pore water pressure (Ueycess)

For sandy soils, the Uexcess is dissipated rapidly due to high permeability and immediate drainage
Therefore, sand experiences immediate settlement

When saturated clay is loaded, elastic settlement, if any, occurs instantly

Due to low permeability and poor drainage, the u.,-¢,, increases

Dissipation of u,,-¢,, through drainage takes a lot of time

Flow is laminar

Associated consolidation settlement continues for a long period of time

Volume of water expelled is equal to the volume change in soil

Since soil is considered as semi-infinite, change in volume is same as change in depth
Time of consolidation depends on the following factors

1. Rate of application of load (cumulative pore water pressure)
2. Coefficient of permeability of soil

3. Drainage path available i.e. one way or two way drainage
